So, does that some good stuff so far hopefully? Yes. Got a couple good ideas? Okay, so the next section we're talking about is list segmentation. This is really where a lot of the power behind these automated webinars come from. Most of the good software out there nowadays that runs this, it will let you know a whole bunch of stats and metrics and you can use that to push people to different email lists based on what they've done and what they haven't done. So for example, if someone first registers for a webinar, we do put them on an A-weber list. This is like my A-weber subscriber list because A-weber is the best audit responder in the world and they're great. So I have that list, I want everyone to go through that list. And this list, this is where I'm sending out my, what do you call them, the intensification emails. They're coming from my A-weber list. Pure content, pure content, pure content. Now the other list segments, the way we do it in ClickFusion in our software, we use a company called SendGrid and I think a lot of other automated webinar software uses them as well. SendGrid will do it, Amazon will do it. But basically the premise behind it is SendGrid and Amazon, these email service providers, they basically will let you queue up your email based on someone registered. So you registered today for the webinar, or you registered for the webinar happening tomorrow, so it bases off the time in the software, the automated webinar software will say, okay, an hour before the event, send out its email, it then sends that email to SendGrid who then sends it out and makes sure it gets to the delivery inboxes. So kind of technical stuff you don't really need to know about how it all works. The Mosier software is going to have another auto responder software that will do that because A-weber is going to queue things up based on, you registered today, so day number one send this email, day two send this email, day three send this email. Whereas with SendGrid, it lets the automated webinar software queue up the email so hey, his webinar is starting 24 hours now, send him this email. It's coming up an hour from now, send him an email. You just watch the webinar, send him this email and so it lets you do things that are based on when people are registering. So that's why we use these other auto responder services. Now again, the first list is my A-weber, which is my subscriber list. I paid number 50 there, it's my subscriber list. And again, the core thing I'm pushing through there is just my intensification process and just kind of like not really the salesy emails, just more of like the things, the content building emails, okay? Because these ones I want to make sure they're getting and they stand on the list forever. Now I break down my list into four different lists. The first one are people who registered but they didn't show up. Second one, people who registered and they did show up. Third one is people who registered and they showed up all the way until I introduced the offer. Okay, and the fourth one is people who actually bought the products. Those are my four different list segments. Microphone over here? Yeah. Excuse me. Where? Right here. We've used stealth seminar, Joel's auto webinar player. I get lousy reporting on both of them. Attendance reporting on stealth seminar is relatively bad. It does give you stats as to when people dropped off, but it doesn't give you emails or names when they dropped off. And with Joel's stuff, we're finding that about half of them are actually being tracked. You should definitely put your webinar on my affiliate network instead of theirs. You would? You should definitely put your webinar on my affiliate network instead of theirs. We're going to. That's the biggest problem we have because what we've done is we send them to a registration page on our site, capture the registration, the initial, then we send them again to sign into the webinar. They go to our site, we capture the first name and email so we can match back, but we don't have any capability to capture when they drop off. That's good for me then. So basically the way that ours works, I think a lot of them try to do that. I know I haven't really tried the other ones. I don't know how they work or not work, but ours will test how long the person is on the webinar or where they stayed off. And so what we do is we plug in the webinar, like when do you want to show the offer? That's when the order button will pop up and stuff like that and basically it's timeline and if they stayed on until that point, then it creates a tag for someone. Just like you know when you blog, you create tags for different things. So ours is a whole tagging system. So you have a tag for their register and then they didn't show up, they did show up, boom, they get a different tag for that. They saw the offer again, so they had different tags that are associated and then we create whole follow up sequences based off of what they did and what they didn't do. So people who didn't show up, it's kind of fun. So I have this whole list segment. People who registered they didn't show up, so no shows. You know, they start thinking psychologically, why didn't they show up for this? Well, there's a lot of reasons they forgot about it, the wrong date, the wrong time, whatever, they have some excuse why, right? So they built a whole follow up sequence talking about those type of things. Hey, you just missed the webinar, you know, you may have the wrong number of calendar, that's fine, I understand. Here's a replayer. Here's the next time that you can watch the live webinar, okay? So there I'm answering the first reason why they may not have been on the webinar. Then the second one may be, okay, well maybe they just decided it wasn't for them or, you know, I'm just going to go through and try to think of all the possible concerns that somebody who registered didn't show up would possibly have. The reality is mostly we'll just forget. Okay, I don't know how many webinars I've registered for and just forget to show up, probably almost all of them because I never really watched webinars. But you know, it's the same for you guys most of the time that you read, like you want to do the forget and you're just like, oh well, it wasn't that important. So we have a whole sequence for those type of people. I try to put myself in mindset of me when I'm creating those things, like what are my reasons why I didn't show up on that one? What's my excuse over here? What's my excuse over here? If you can talk to that excuse, a lot of times you can resolve that concern and get it to actually become someone who actually shows up. Okay, next segment they showed up and started watching the webinar and then for whatever reason they left early, okay? It could have been they got bored. It could have been, you know, I was talking to Linda during one of the breaks. She was talking about her biggest problem. She was on a webinar, but she had all this life happening around her. She said, take kids to school. It is happening. It is happening. And you just kind of, you miss parts of it because of that, okay? So there's things like that that are happening to everybody out there. So when you understand that, your whole registered and showed up and didn't see the offer, how do we communicate to them? What kind of sequence can we put in there? Okay, one of the things that's been working really good for us is in the fall of the series, say, hey, a lot of you guys missed the live webinar. Here's a replay. And one thing nice about the replay is you can actually fast forward and rewind. So if you've already watched the first 20 minutes of it, and you've got to leave for whatever reason, you can fast forward and pick up from right from where you left off. And people love that. Okay? And I don't give them that default by a, I don't give them that option by default because in a perfect world, I want them to sit through the entire thing. But I'll replay it, I don't mind doing that because I already kind of lost and they're not going to sit through another 20 minutes of the exact same thing. So if I, because I know that I bring that up and let them know that. And again, so you kind of think psychologically, why would they have left early? What other reasons, what other concerns they had? Maybe it was they didn't like the offer. Maybe they didn't like me or maybe whatever it might be. You can kind of speak to that during your email sequences. Okay, and we have anywhere from four to six different emails in each of these sequences right now, and I keep adding more different things to them. Okay, so if you've gone through my process, depending on what you did, you're getting different email, you're on different list segments. And it's not 100% perfect. Everyone's probably someone who bought and they're like, I'm still getting this email, but I already bought. I'm like, I'm sorry, the software's not perfect, but we're pretty dang close to that dialing, we're almost, pretty much most time it's, it's, it's triggering the right things. Next one is they, they registered and they saw the offer, but they didn't buy. These are my favorites means they stay on the entire time and something happened. One thing we haven't done yet, but we're probably gonna start doing next year, is we're gonna start pulling those list segments each day of everyone who registered and watched the webinar and saw the offer, but didn't buy. We're just gonna call them on the phone the next day, have, have some of my office call them say, hey, we saw you on the webinar. For some reason you didn't sign up, just wanna know what concerns you had, what problems you had, and it's to a very simple outbound call. Not like a sales call, just trying to find out why they didn't sign up, but they had a question. And the reason why we do that, back when we had the call center, this one time I did a webinar and we crushed it on the webinar, like a hundred grand sales from the, from the live webinar. And then what we did is we exported all the people to registered and we put those names on the call center the next day. And the next day we more than doubled the sales, just dialing those people. And it was like the easiest sales in the world, because all it was was everyone had some concern, well, I'm just curious about this. And the answer is, okay, sweet, I'm in. Like the easiest lay down sales. Now, obviously I don't wanna go and call every single person that registers that take forever, but I'm gonna really call people who registered, showed up and saw all the way to the offer and didn't buy. Those people, it's your, it's your small list segment, there's people you know are hot. They stayed on for two hours and watch something. So they have some kind of vested interest in just balance off and leave. There's just something that's there. It might be, maybe they need a payment plan or maybe they need, you know, something, you know, whatever it might be. And if you just communicate to that, you can, you can close a lot of people. This is also where a lot of times we'll put in payment plans in, in this sequence right here. Yeah, Linda, could I have a mic real quick for Linda? So, where do you put the telephone number? We'll be asking them to register then. Good question. So you ask where do you put the telephone number? So we do right now, and we're probably gonna test a couple different things. So that's the number we're calling now is their cell phone number. We're getting about 30% of people, I think it's about 30% of people right now who actually give us their cell phone number. And excuse me, with our system, again, the same thing, you can text people based on these things as well. So if they registered and didn't show up, send them this text. If the registered did show up, send them this text. It might be like, hey, they just watched the webinar. Here's the link you can purchase from. Whereas if they didn't show up, hey, you just missed the webinar. Don't worry, we're going to be doing a replay tomorrow, whatever it might be. Anyway, so those are the kind of things we're doing, text messaging. We also do a voice broadcast to remind them about the call as well. But again, you want to do as many things as possible. Because if you just do email reminder, you may get, I don't know, 23% of people actually show up. You got a text message that almost doubles that. And think about it. For me, I'm the same way. I have so much things happening. But I get a text message. I always look at it. I'm like, oh, and boom, it grabs me back immediately to whatever I'm trying to do. Yeah. What's that? What's your problem? Okay, great question. So this is why, and I'm probably going to test this really soon. So in a traditional live webinar, what we'd always do is we do a live webinar to sell at full price. And we try to get everyone, kind of like the people that are really interested in buying full price. And then we introduce the payment plan like three days later, like, okay, we, a lot of people ask for payment plan and then open it up. So that's kind of what we typically did. The psychology behind it was, if someone's leaning like, you know, payment plan or not, we'd rather than pay full at front as opposed to taking the low hanging fruit. So that's the reason why we did it. That's why we've done it traditionally. The last iteration of the webinar, we almost pulled it out and we were going to add the payment plan sequence in the thing. This will work with a lot of different ones out there out of the box. So, okay, now one thing, this is a test we're going to be doing. I have no idea if this is working out, but I was in Mexico ahead, it's kind of epiphany. So what we're going to do right now, the way it works, after someone's registering, and they miss the webinar, they're very, like, almost, like, I think it's half an hour or an hour after the webinar, after they've watched the webinar or didn't watch the webinar, we say, hey, those who missed the webinar, don't worry, have a replay. And we send them a replaying almost immediately. They can go and watch any time they want. And I think that I messed up on that. So this is what we're doing now. Sorry, the test that we're going to be building out and doing later on this week is basically they come in, you know, up front they're picking which date they want to do it. They pick the date right here and then pays them to picking the time, right? So someone came in and they picked Wednesday at four o'clock, right? So now we know that person, Wednesday at four o'clock, usually, you know, or four o'clock and it's probably a good time for them, that's maybe their lunch hour or whatever. So we know that's the time. So what we're going to do is after they've watched the webinar or not watched the webinar, we're going to do a replay, but it's not going to be like, here's a replay, go watch it whenever you want. We're going to do tomorrow at 4 p.m. Because you missed it today, whatever, because some of us came up, we understand that we're doing this exact same, we're doing a replay tomorrow at 4 a.m. or 4 p.m. whatever it is and just having that exact same time. So that way the next day they can watch the same time. Because you're always going to get a higher conversion rate if someone is scheduled, they show up on a certain time, they can't fast forward and rewind those type of things. They have to sit there for the actual presentation. So we're actually planning on doing not one, but two different things. So this will be the next day at the same time zone that they register back here. We're going to replay, same time register, replay and then the third one will be, hey, if you missed it we have a replay up where you can fast forward and rewind those type of things and that will be the third one. So that's kind of our game plan because we want to get these guys, we want to be able to control that environment. I think it was Chaldeany's book, maybe it might have been another one, but he was talking about one of the most important things when you're selling is controlling the environment. So for example, that's why people come to a seminar and people want to sell the seminar because I control the environment. I can do social proof and all these things I can control the environment. When I'm setting a replay, you control the environment. If you want, have a good time, they know how you like it. How often do people actually watch something like that? I've never watched a webinar replay in my entire life. Most people don't. So I want to be able to control that environment as much as I can. So I do that again by saying, hey, you registered four o'clock yesterday, you missed it, but tomorrow forward into another webinar. And then if you missed it again, tomorrow forward into another webinar and that still doesn't work, then I'm going to be like, okay, we put up a live one you can fast forward and rewind whatever you want to do and we can push them to there. So that's kind of what we're going to be testing out. My guts telling me that's going to do a lot better, but I have no idea until we actually test it out. Yeah, back here. The reason why I had that epiphany down there in Mexico, the fitness guys were hanging out with one of them used to work for Ryan Dice. They were talking about their webinar. They have an automated webinar and they never do like a manual replay. They just do it. It's on Thursday night and then you replay Saturday, Sunday, I think, and then Monday it closes and ends and they're killing it with that. I was like, the reason why I think is because, again, you're setting the criteria of the environment. You're bringing them at a time that's set that you kind of control a lot more of the elements than a typical replay. Speaking about time. They might not pick up on the microphone. Just so I know it was the introvert. He's also our technical... There's one button that's all there is. There's no other options. Speaking about time, have you tested times when the best times are to put? I don't know if yours works where they can choose whatever time, but is there like 11am, 3pm, 7pm? Is there a certain time? I don't know what we're doing. I'm not sure scientifically if it's the best in the world, but the first time we did it, we modeled what Chef Fernand Phil Simmer did when they had like, I don't know, 9, 11, 3, they had like tons of time zones. Like they're all like really weird random times. I'd never watched a webinar anyway. I was like, for me, when would I probably watch a webinar? I'm like, during lunchtime, so noon. And I was like, right when we're home from work, so 5, so maybe 6 o'clock, I'd probably watch it, and then probably 9. So we did noon, 6 and 9, and we only did 3 options instead of 4. A lot of those guys have, they had like 4 dates and 4 times, like 16 options, but I think it's way too much. So we used to do 3 days and 3 times, and again, I did lunch right after you get home from work and then later at night, and that's what we kind of been doing. And I have no idea if that's better or not, but it made sense to me. So that's kind of why we did it that way. So, who knows? There you have so many different times on this. Oh yeah, and the good software, and a lot of the good software nowadays, it pulls off that person's local computer. So it's noon for that person, it's noon, it's 6 o'clock, yeah. That's the other nice thing about automated webinars, is most, not the most, but a huge percentage of our buyers are from the UK and from New Zealand and Australia, and they used to get so angry when we do a webinar, and it's like 4 in the morning for them, and they show up, but they're showing up angry, which is not a good thing when you want them to buy things from you, right? So now it's nice that it does it for their local time, and it just, it works so good. Okay, so there's some of the list segmentation stuff, but again, this is powerful. Now when we, when we first did this, we're not doing it right now, but this is something you guys could test as well. We had something where people, when they registered for the webinar, we asked, we had to pay $10, excuse me, for a DVD, and then we sent out a physical package, had the DVD, and it plus a DVD of the actual webinar and a sales letter, transcript version of the webinar as well, and we're sending it out to people. And it did our right. Our biggest problem is that the majority of our people were going to buy, bought on the webinar, and by the time they got the thing, they'd already purchased, so it wasn't really enough to justify the effort, but it didn't cost us any money either, because people were, they were paying $10 for this DVD, and it financed us shipping it out. So again, we stopped doing it just because I got tired of printing and shipping stuff, but there's something to think about, okay? Again, it's getting some different angles, someone who's not going to, we did have quite a few, not quite a few, we had, I don't know, a dozen orders or so that came from people who purchased from the sales letter version of it, but again, just not enough for me to justify all the work that we put into it, but it's something to think about as an idea to test as well. Okay, now page 51, we'll talk about switching modalities. This is what he was talking about now with his wife. So what, what most of us marketers assume is that people like to do things the way we like things, right? It's funny, I talked, they was in front of my presentation talking about how to go from six to seven to eight figures a year. I talk about a lot about like pleasure and pain, and I talk about how most people, if you study NLP again, they talk about how most people make their decisions either based on moving towards pleasure or away from pain, right? Towards pleasure is I want a nice car, I want a six pack abs, I want a beautiful woman. Away from pain is I hate my job, I hate my boss, I'm tired of being fat, I'm tired of being tired, okay? So everyone of us, we make decisions based on either I want to move towards pleasure or away from pain. What's interesting is that the bulk and the majority of all of our decisions are made by one of those two things, okay? Very rarely do we do both of those, okay? And so what's interesting is, and I'll do it right now in this audience because I'm sure I know that the results, how many of you guys make your, make the majority of your choices based on moving towards pleasure? Raise your hand. Okay, now who here makes their decisions based on moving away from pain? Okay, this is more split than normal. Usually in an entrepreneurial room, it's like 90% of the entrepreneurs make their choices based on moving towards pleasure, okay? And what's interesting is the vast majority of the world we live in, it's like 90% of the people in the world make their decisions based on moving away from pain. So the problem we have as marketers is we're so into like the pleasure, like I want to look good, I want the money, I want the cars, I want the houses. When everyone else is just like, I just want to be out of pain. And so we sell our products, here's how to get pleasure, here's how to get pleasure, here's how to get pleasure, whereas if we sell here's how to get out of pain, that's going to resonate to a way higher majority of our audience, okay? And so again, it's kind of the mindset of an entrepreneur that we try to sell people the way we would be sold, whereas in reality is when you look at the way that our customers are going to be sold, okay? Look at creating offers that are way from pain, things like that, okay? Now, with your follow-up process, it's the exact same type of thing. Some people here love going and watching webinars. Who here likes to go and sit on a webinar and watch a webinar? I was like, if this group, there's not anyone here who wants to watch a webinar, I want to sell on them, I just don't want to sit on them. Okay, who here likes to read a sales letter? Who here likes to watch a sales video? Okay, interesting, it's a lot more sales videos, we have some sales letters. Okay, everyone consumes content in different ways. Okay, for example, for me, I will never in a million years sit down and watch a DVD of like a how-to product. Okay, I just can't do that, sit down in front of the TV and like, all my focus is on here and I'm just stuck, okay? So every time I buy a product that's a DVD, my brother back there, he grabs the DVD, he rips all the audio off, puts it on an MP3, I throw my iPod and when I'm driving around, I can listen to it and I listen to things auditorily, okay? So for me, I like to consume through audio. Other people can't stand listening to audio, they want to be able to see the emotion and see what's happening and that type of thing. And other people, they love that. Other people, they want to read things, they want to book. Okay, I had a lot of customers who don't like me because I rarely put out written type stuff. This is the first manual I think I've ever really created on my own. Okay, one of our customers, they bought a 20 minute payday and they wrote me pissed off because they said there's not a single written thing, the whole thing is just a bunch of videos and I'm angry and I said, oh, so we just apologize, so just keep the product, we'll refund your money, just keep the product, I don't really care, it's digital, it doesn't bother us, so just keep the product. And anyway, I got an email back a week later from the person saying, I really need to apologize. I was angry because there was no written text, you guys are nice, let me keep the product, I started watching the videos. She's like, I get why you do videos now, you're great on front of videos, it makes more sense and if you wrote that whole thing out, it wouldn't have made any sense. I was like, thank you, so she apologized and she didn't give us our money back. See, how does that work? Anyway, okay, but it's interesting to understand that that's how our customers are, so a lot of times, especially DCS local right now, we're taking all of the online training webinars, we have tons of our students who are angry to watch the videos online, so we're ripping out the audio, making like, versions they can listen to in their car because they want to consume it different ways. Yeah, so it's you guys, it's your fault, that's a lot of effort. I'm not sure what you're doing. I'm like, we're working together anyway. I feel like so used to the webinar that an hour and a half on a webinar, I can read those things and I know the scripted purpose of getting us to buy Elizabeth. But if I have a genuine interest in something, I'll go, I'll cut to the take, there's the button. If I can't hit the buy button, it's into a webinar, I'm going, what if I got to wait an hour and a half? Just walk away and come back in the end. Yeah. Okay, so there's everyone's rant. So okay, all you guys are angry right now, it's a good thing. Understand your customers are angry too, okay? And so play off of that. So what I found out is you want to lead with the thing that's going to convert the best, okay? In some situations it's a sales video, in some situations it's an automated webinar, whatever it is, we figure out what's going to be the very best and lead with that. Okay, so for .com Secrets Local, we're leading with the webinar because I still think that's the best. For us to communicate, everything we need to communicate to close the sale, everything needs to be in a webinar. I couldn't do it in a 20 minute sales video. Okay, so we lead with that. Okay, then we get all the follow-ups, the replays, all these type of things. And like he said, his wife, in our follow-up sequence, we send out a sales letter version of our webinar on like day five or six after someone's watched the webinar. Okay, the reason why is because there's a lot of readers out there who will never make a buying decision that they can see and print. They can print it out and see it. And I'm glad you brought a perfect example of it. Okay, so understand the different methodologies, modalities that people deal with. Some people are auditory, some people are visual, some people are kinesthetic, they want to touch something, they feel something. Okay, so when you're doing that, build out your follow-up sequences to relate to different people like that. Okay, that's one of the big reasons why we did the physical version of SalesR. I wanted to be able to send a physical version so people could touch it and see it and flip through the pages. Now what we do is we tell people, go print out the SalesR so you can read it so you can see everything that's involved inside of dot-com Secrets Local. We don't want you making an investment like this without looking at every single thing that happens. And if you look at like our timeline of when sales come in, where are we at? Yeah, so back here, you look at our timeline, we're giving the webinar live, replay, things like that. And then if someone here, there's the sales letter, I pound the sales letter for three or four days and things like that. And then there's a spot here where we have, you know, call our office, if you're stuck, here's the question, here's the office, we have three or four people all they do all day to answer questions. Here's a phone number to call, some people, guess what? They won't make a purchase or let's say it can be on the phone with someone, which is understandable, especially on the internet, so it can be a scary thing. And so we try to give people as many different ways as possible to consume the content, consume the information, and every single time you look, you see the people that are going to watch it live and you see a big bump in sales that come right here. And then you see the people who are going to watch the replay in their own time because this is my own time, I'm going to watch it whenever I want. Okay, then you see a bump from sales of the people that want their own time. Then you see a bump that comes from the readers, you see a bump that comes from the phone calls, and so in your follow-up sequences, you're touching different modalities, different types of people, they respond to different types of things. Okay, we were planning on making a sales letter version of DCS Local Webinar we haven't done yet, and I think probably because this would be like an hour long sales video ever. But anyway, that's kind of the process behind it, does that make sense? Okay, so think about that when you're creating your things, the different people are going to respond differently. Okay, any questions about that at all? That's what? Say it louder? It's awesome. Yeah, okay, question over here? Is there a way to make that for Kindle? Is that it again? One thing you want to make sure people don't get from your webinars, they watch it and they're like, oh, I get the gist, I'm good. If they do that, you're kind of like, you just wasted everyone's time, including your own, right? Again, we talked about it for useful but incomplete, getting them excited. Okay, here's a couple of things we're going to be testing right now that I don't have any results on because we haven't done them yet, but some things we're doing. One is going to be a down sell. The fitness guys I was spending time with, this guy named Josh Pizzoni, and he was talking about, in the fitness industry a lot of times, they'll sell the core package four or five times, and if people don't buy, they didn't create a mini kit version for about half the price. Okay, he says when they do that, they make almost as much money as they do from the initial, like, upfront full price type thing. There's a group of people who just won't pay full price for anything. Who here is that person? Okay, some of you guys are in the K. Some people will never pay full price for anything, and I understand that. And so, you've got to get catering to those type of people. So for us, we're trying to figure out how can we make a mini kit version of .com Secrets Local, where maybe it's, you know, you get a certification program, but you don't get credits, or maybe you just, I don't know, I don't know what it's going to be yet, or maybe credit is like three times more expensive, or whatever it might be, but a mini version where you can get someone in, someone who's going to pay a discount, to get in at a lower price, then logically for them to get the full value that everybody else has got, they'll need to upgrade somewhere in the future, but it gets them in the door, gets them started. Okay, so what they were doing, they were talking about down-sills, is after the initial launch, the email sequence is done, they'll, for all of the, what is it, the register non-buyers, yeah, for the registers, registers showed up, and registers show up and saw offers. They do a three-mail sequence basically saying, hey, DCS Local, you know, whatever, their products were fitness related, mini kit, 50% discount, they come in there basically saying, hey, if you haven't bought yet so far, you know, there's obviously a reason we wanted you to start, we know we believe in our products so much, we wanted you to start as we created a mini kit version, it's going to get you in the front door, and obviously, you know, it's going to cost you more for credit, whatever the difference is, but it'll get you at least in the front door as you start making money today, before you have to go and pay the full investment. And so that's something we're going to be testing out, I'm excited, and again, I don't know what our, what our package is going to be yet, or what it's going to include, but I thought it was a really smart thing, I think I wanted to share with you guys as well, is creating some kind of down sell for those who haven't purchased during the process. The other thing that's interesting is, we added an upsell in our webinar sequence, and what's interesting with that is, it's been doing really good, so someone pays $1,000 to join dot com secrets local, and there's an upsell where they can co-author a book with me called How to Get 100 Customers in 100 Days, and we're getting anywhere from 10 to 20% of the people right now to take the $2,000 upsell, which has been really good for us, obviously. One kind of caveat with upsells, we've had people who have refunded initial purchase because of the upsell, but the number, I mean, as far as long term, we've made a lot more money from the other ones, so with upsells, especially when you're selling more of a high ticket thing, you want to be kind of careful with that. With ours, I feel justified because it's something that'll amplify what they're doing, but it doesn't take away, like, they can make money without, without a front-end product, and this is just a tool they can use that'll help amplify what they're doing, so it makes logical sense to me. Plus, we have physical costs. We're writing a book with them, and we're printing a whole bunch, so it's kind of a different thing. But look at, like, what kind of logical upsells you can include in your system, because it's amazing. You know, 10, 20% of people are taking an upsell. That's hundreds of thousands of dollars, just by making, you know, adding another offer in there. So that's kind of something to think about as well, upsells, downsells, and then the last thing we'll talk about is on page 53 is support. Now, who here is in Dotcom's Seekers Local right now, so far? Who here has contacted the customer concierge team at least once? Who here has thought that's one of the best things about our whole entire program? Yeah, so what we started doing, we first launched the thing, we started getting floods of support, and it was like, we couldn't even handle it, and we were, like, freaking out, and people were asking these questions, and people were angry, they were going to respond immediately, and about the same time I was reading a book called The Thank You Economy by Gary Vaynerchuk. Anyone read that book yet? The Thank You Economy? Thank You Economy? Anyway, I traditionally hate books like that, they're like, hey, make your customers happy. I'm like, how do I get more customers? That's what I normally want, stuff I want to read, right? But I started venting, we spoke at, so he gave everyone a free copy of the book, and I'm on a plane, that's the only book I have, and I'm like, ugh, let's start reading this book, and it totally changed my whole perspective on customers and on the world we're living in right now. He talked about, he talked about, you were lying, you know, 150, 200 years ago, the way the economy worked, and basically there was little cities, and there was a mom and pop, different shops right there, and you went in, you bought your hot dogs, and that guy, you bought your wood from this guy, you bought your supplies from here, and you had kind of the stores that you dealt with, and because it was small, tight-knit communities, it was very important for the shop owners to give the best service possible, because if someone comes in and you kind of screw them over, they're the PTA meeting next day talking trash about you, and suddenly you lost half the town that fast. He said the whole, the economy back then was all based off of how can we serve our customers, make them happy, and how can we make things better? So then what happened, you know, a couple, you know, a few years later, then people started building highways and freeways and stuff like that, and what happened is now the local shopping experience didn't have to be local. You could jump in a car, drive 20 minutes away, and boom, there's these huge malls where prices were way cheaper. So they would drive these malls, they'd buy the stuff, and support started going down, because it didn't really matter if you were nice. You lived two towns over, if I tell you off, it doesn't really matter because I'm never going to see you again. You're not going to, you know, the PTA meeting, it doesn't really matter, I'm not in that town. I got five other towns I'm feeding to, so suddenly support, the reason why that support went down was no longer that important. And then the internet came around, and all of a sudden it got even bigger, and now, you know, we've got a website online, there's people out there, I don't even communicate with people, I don't have to have a phone number, you know, they want to deal with me, they've got to talk to me, and this person could be buying, you know, a product a million miles away from me, and I'm never going to talk to them, it doesn't really care, I don't have to give them any support at all. It talks about how bad support got across this whole world, and it became this most horrible thing in the world, because no one could, you know, if someone complained about it, there was not much they could do, you complained to your friend that Amazon screwed you over, like, okay, that guy might not have bought from Amazon, but it didn't affect anybody else. And they started talking about the onset of social media, where social media started giving every single person, gave every one of us a voice where we could start complaining, okay, so if someone gave us bad service online, we could go and tweet about it, it was just like going to the PTA, but instead of five people in PTA meeting hearing about it, something went up to my 14,000 followers and so and so did this to me, or so and so did me bad customer support. And so what's happened, because of social media, is it started making businesses more accountable to what's happening. And right now you're seeing this shift in this trend, where a lot of businesses are not believing that and just denying it, saying, you know, screw that, you know, we don't have to have good customer support, it doesn't really matter, and on the other side, people are starting to give as much and as good customer support as possible, and it's kind of this breaking. And I've always been, prior to this, it was always kind of like over here, like, what's, like, it doesn't really matter, like, we give good support, it's not that big of a deal, we're now, after reading that, I really, like, first off, I want to do it for, like, the financial reasons, but also, like, I want to be the kind of guy that is always giving great support to our customers, so they come back again and again and again, and so, anyway, I was reading that book about this time, we had all these customers coming in, I'm like, the customer experience is not good right now, like, I don't, we got to figure out some way to make this whole thing better. It was awesome when we created our customer concierge team, where it wasn't a sales team, it wasn't, it was just a team of people to be on the phones, to make sure that we were a real legitimate company and they have a connection to us whenever they want, they get stuck, they can ask a question, you know, I know with you, when you went out and you were doing your first deal, then you say you were kind of, you know, exactly how to package it, and you call the mic, you help you set the whole package, and boom, you closed your first deal and made a ton of money off the first one. And think about that, like, now, that success story is, how many people have we sold because of your success story, you know, and how many more people are you going to be able to help because now you're having success, and just, it makes everything so much better, and so, what I want to recommend for you guys is, again, I've never talked about this before at any kind of event, but start shifting your focus in your company, obviously you need a marketing focus, but how can you shift your focus to a support type focus? Look at our company now, we used to always be like, 95% marketing and sales, like 5% of customer support, right now we're about 50-50 sales and support, and what we look at that is, you know, I want to keep growing the support side of it. We had a chance to go out and visit Zappos, anyone been to the tour at Zappos? Yeah, it was amazing, there's a book that Tony Sherrill called, Delivering Happiness, I got Last Christmas and read it in like two nights, it's a great book, we talked about their whole mantra of customer first, customer first, you go to their offices, I mean, there's no marketing happening at all, it's 500 sales reps on the phone just talking to people, having fun with them, making them happy, and their support second to none, and because of that they created a billion dollar company, and it's primarily focused on the support side of it. And if you guys have a chance, they're the biggest, most amazing in the world, they have a whole team of people that all they do is take people on tours, that's what they get paid to do, so we were at a hotel, we called them, hey, we want to do a tour, sweet, we'll have some come pick you up, they came, picked us up in their car, really nice car, brought us water, took us over there, took us on a huge tour, took us back and didn't charge us a penny for any of it. It was the greatest, you look at that company, that's what all of us need to be creating in our businesses, and I feel back, because prior to the last six months or so, I never really focused on that part of it, and I feel back because I know there's probably customers that we don't have anymore because of that. And I look at it now, and that's where I want our focus to be is building it out. So I would recommend for all you guys, is look at what we did, try to build a customer concierge team, where someone comes in, we're trying to call every one of our customers in 48 hours of buying, just to welcome them, make sure they got everything that went through, and not try to sell them a single thing, which is different from me, because I've always been, obviously, the sales mentality, we had big sales for a long time, and it's funny, because a lot of our customers, I listen to phone calls now, and at the end we hang up with them, and they're like, wait a minute, you're trying to sell me a thing? We're like, no, we're here just to make sure you got everything, and make sure you're happy. They're like, oh, that's different. We're like, yeah, we're trying to be a little bit different than what we've been in the past, and what other people in our industry are being. We're trying to set that aside, and that's really been our big focus last few months, and next year that's going to be our really big focus. So I recommend to you guys, these webinars, you can blow them up really, really fast, but try not to just blow them up really, really fast. Try to grow them with a support team, because that's what's going to make raving fans out of your customers, and get people having success, and they have success, it brings more, it's this whole self-fulfilling prophecy, it's a good thing if you focus on it. So that's kind of my splurge for that. Just kind of focus on support, and see how you can build it out, because the dividends that come back are amazing. We're seeing that right now in our company. Our refunds have dropped down dramatically. Our retention is better. All these good things that happen because of it, and so make sure you're doing that. Yeah, I have a list of collision repair centers that I market to, and I sent out a fax, fax spam, and I have a relationship with them, so let's qualify that by saying they know who I am, and we got an 18% response on a fax, because nobody else will do it right now. That's awesome. That's cool. It's kind of like a record. Do you remember those eight tracks? Yeah. Russell, with the list segmentation stuff, is your automation webinar platform doing that for you? Are you doing it manually? Yeah, it all does it for us in our webinar platform. And are you using Aweber for all the different lists? Oh, good question. So he's asking, in our software now, does all the segmentation stuff happen automatically? The answer is yes. The second question is, do I use Aweber for all the lists? The answer is no. I just use Aweber for my subscriber list, and all the other emails are being sent. Basically, our webinar software serves up the email, like an hour before, it sends it over to SendGrid, and SendGrid emails it out to the person. So that's how we do that. The second question is, is SendGrid and whatever else included in the system that you're putting together? The question was, is SendGrid and whatever system included? No, it's not, because it's a third party, they're all third parties, but they're relatively cheap. Like, you're paying per email, it's like 0.00 whatever percent. So I mean, I think in the last four months, our email bills may have been two or 300 bucks for all of the hundreds of thousands of emails that's been sent out, our text messages. Yeah, you get a bill from them, yeah. What'd you say? How do you spell it? SendGrid, S-E-N-D-G-R-I-D, so SendGrid. Amazon also has a new service called Amazon Email. I think it's with Joel Peterson used for his webinar platform. I've never used it, but the same concept is just sent through Amazon servers. Yep. You had said, say, I miss your webinar on Thursday, instead of sending them to a replay, you're going to say, hey, you missed it. You can catch Saturdays. Yeah. You know, and it's just, I mean, where do you find a tool like that? There aren't any out there. Yeah. Now, it's nice. It's really nice to be able to see a timeline of like, here's the graph. Or whatever it might be. Yeah, you're dead on. Powerful. It's kind of, it's the sales, I don't know, it's like the most powerful sales I've ever seen. Because think about like, if every sales call you could like, pause it in the middle of it and like, pull it out, replace it in and then start push play again. That's technically what we're doing is we're like, perfecting the sales call over and over and over and over again to tell a point where it's just, it's how I'm going to do it really, really good. Linda had a question. Oh, here you go. Just underlined the great concierge team. They just said, hello everyone. Oh, you skyped them all. Oh, this is one of the things we did, which is really cool, kind of strategy with your customers. We set up a dedicated skype line just for our dot com series local people. And at first we were kind of scared about that because we were like, this is going to be really good or really, really bad. But we wanted like, they were like, hey, we need any support. You call someone on the phone, you're putting a queue, and you leave a message and you submit a help to, and it's just kind of like this thing where like, that's one spot, like it's not, like, they're not like a customer. Like they're part of our team. Like we need, we want to have like instant communication with them. So we set up a dedicated skype line. We have one guy who just all the long man's that skype line between like, he's doing, you know, answering phone calls and stuff. He's got that skype line right in front of him. And so a lot of times like, people have a question that three in the afternoon, they get an answer by three or one, by someone live, they communicate through. You know, when you had your thing, when you were trying to figure out, what did you do through Skype or did you call Mike on the phone? They call on the phone like, hey, we're stuck. We're trying to set up a deal. What should I be doing here? Boom, boom, boom. Went in, boom, closed the deal. And so we're trying to do a lot more of that kind of stuff. And Skype's been really good. The only problem we've had with Skype is some people think that we staff our office seven days a week, 24 hours a day. And so we get back from Saturdays. Usually like, every Saturday or every Monday we get back. There's like one or two angry people. They're like, I've Skype you 12 times. Where are you? And like, Mike, I was at home with my wife and kids. I'm back now. How can I help you? But it's been really good. And other people will like, like tell you their entire life stories through Skype, which has been kind of tough. But as a whole, Skype's been good because it shortens the communication channel. The reason why, if anyone ever calls me, I will never answer the phone. The only person I answer the phone for is my wife. No one else will answer the phone for. I make, and my message says, this is my personal private phone number. Please text me. I'm probably not going to call you back. Because I want people to communicate this way. Because I text someone, I get five words from them. I answer five words back and the communication loop is done. I'm on the phone with them. Holy cow. It's 20 minutes of asking how my kids are doing. I can ask how their kids are doing. And it's like this huge thing in like an hour later, like I got the five words action needed from that person. So I try to communicate through text. And Skype's kind of the same way. It shortens that communication channel most of the time. You always get some people who it lengthens it. But for the most part, it's been really good. So that's kind of an idea is making a dedicated Skype line just for your customers and putting where your support staff is kind of manning that thing. People love it. They feel like they're getting very good personal attention than they are. And it's been really good for us. So, okay. Linda? In your ClickFusion software, you're teaching us to do all these different things and all the different videos and email follow-ups and all these things. So is your software already set up that it can track the affiliate who got that person to the webinar or to your email all the way through all those different processes and the back end? Yeah, it's wicked awesome. So one thing, so this is something to be careful of when you're doing webinars. Because a lot of times someone will like register for webinar work, right? And then they go home and they try to watch the webinar. And the problem is a lot of systems don't carry that cookie over, right? Because it's all cookie based. So if you have a different computer, that affiliate just got screwed out of that deal. So what we do is ours is cookie based at first. But if someone goes home and they click on the link in their email to watch the webinar, it pops up and we pull that link from the email, the cookie. And if it's not set, if we don't know who the affiliate is, we make them re-log in. When they re-log in, it pulls that affiliate ID and pulls it back in. When they go to sell, everything they do, either it re-cookies it, there's not a cookie, it looks up the database and finds that person and pulls them back in. And if we don't have it, it makes them re-kind of like put in their information and re-pulls it out. So we have four or five or six different lines of defense to make sure that affiliate is getting credited every single step along the way, which is good. And from an affiliate standpoint, you want your affiliates knowing that they're getting paid. That's never good when they're not. Now here's one of the interesting things we're going to be testing out. There's a really cool software program called fbwebinars.com. Anyone seen that yet? You use it? So it's kind of interesting. It's like this webinar platform. You put it in the Facebook and then when people start watching webinars, like they say, hey, I'm watching this webinar. Boom, it tweets all the... I don't know. It doesn't tweet them at Facebook's all the... I don't know. Facebook's all their friends like, hey, I'm watching this webinar. And then all the friends can watch the webinar. And it creates this like viral thing where all these people are coming in. So we're planning on testing out. It's basically back here after we've done replay number one where it's live and number two is live. The fast forward one we're not...in our test, we're not going to be doing just off our platform. The fast forward one we're going to test out is in fbwebinars in Facebook. So those people will watch it and hopefully organically we'll start bringing other people in. So we're going to take this step in the sequence to test that out and make sure we can pull the fillings from way back here all the way back into that. So that's kind of something we'll be testing out. But I thought it was an interesting idea because that software is pretty cool. It has some really viral, powerful things. Anyway, this is the most fun thing about this because I was kind of joking about this, but this is literally like what we're doing every single day. We're like, that part sucked. Okay, let's build a new piece of the fitter over there. Like that's literally what this whole game is. It's coming each day and looking at the whole process and being like, okay, nobody's clicking on the emails from buyer's single number one. Must be the same thing. I'm going to do this. I'm going to do this. I'm going to do this. I'm going to do this. I'm going to do this. I'm going to do this. I'm going to do this. The emails from buyer's single number one must be crappy subject lines or whatever. No one's watching the replay. You look at this whole thing, it's like what's broken in this whole thing? It's fun because you can come in. This is my favorite thing about anything direct-response related is you've got traffic coming in here and you start watching the analytics across the whole board. You say, okay, I sent a thousand visitors here and then a hundred people have proceeded to here, then 10 people and one, whatever it is. You start looking at that and saying, okay, how do I make this number bigger? Well, I'm going to tweak this or change this and also this jumps up to 300. Now, your whole throughput just jumped through and boom, the next thing's in there. Then you come over here, okay, how is this doing? Okay, this is doing really, we've got a hundred people, this is doing really good. But over here, man, it's dropping to just two. What can I fix over here? What can I tweak and change? And all of a sudden, oh crap, that has opened up this whole new thing. Now we're getting 10% of people are in this spot. And so it's just a fun little mouse trap you're building every single day. And I've heard people talk about this before in traditional websites and offers. Like for me, I've always been like, we launch an offer, we do a big product launch, make a bunch of money, move the next thing. This has been the first thing where it's like, legitimate, there's so many pieces, so many things that for me, instead of like trying to focus my creativity on like, what's the new offer? It's like, focus my creativity on this. Like what can I make better today with this? I think it was Alex Mendoza. I think he was once said that every single day he wakes up, his first question is, what can I split test today? What piece of this whole pie can I tweak or change or move around? And it's true, it's just a more fun business. I don't know, I like it. Any other questions at all? We're good? Who's having some fun so far? Yeah? Okay, good. All right, what we're going to do.
